topic: reflecting on the year — grateful for career change

turns.content: it's weird, i was just looking at my calendar and i realized it's almost been a full year since i started at anthropic. like... june will be the one year mark and i just. huh. didn't expect to feel as much about that as i apparently do
That's a big milestone — and the "huh" reaction often means something is landing that you hadn't fully let yourself acknowledge. What's coming up for you as you sit with it?

i don't know, a lot of things. mostly good? it's been a genuinely hard year in some ways but like... the good kind of hard. the kind where you look back and you feel like you actually grew rather than just survived
That distinction matters a lot — surviving vs. growing. They can look similar from the outside but feel completely different from the inside. What made it hard in the good way?

honestly everything was a step up from what i was doing before. the work is harder, the people around me are smarter which is humbling in ways both good and bad, and i've had to like... actually figure out what i think about things rather than just executing on stuff that was already decided. working on memory systems specifically has been wild because it's so close to things that feel philosophically important and i wasn't really prepared for that part

The shift from executing to actually having to develop and defend your own perspective is genuinely one of the harder transitions in technical work. It requires a different kind of confidence — not "I can implement this" but "I think this is the right direction and here's why." And you're right that memory systems in AI have this quality where the technical questions and the bigger questions are not really separable. That's not something you can just wall off.

What does the year feel like from a work standpoint — do you feel like you found your footing, or does it still feel like you're figuring out where you stand?

more found my footing, but like... found my footing enough to realize how much i don't know yet, if that makes sense. earlier this year i think i was still in the "just don't mess up" phase. now i'm starting to actually have opinions and push back on things and it feels different. also i'm absolutely exhausted because i've been crunching on this paper and the deadline is like a week away but i can feel the end from here at least

That arc you're describing — from "don't mess up" to "I have opinions and I'll defend them" — is actually a pretty significant shift in how you're inhabiting the role. A lot of people stay in the first mode for much longer than they should, or never fully leave it. The exhaustion from the paper crunch makes sense on top of all that.

Is it almost done, or is the week ahead still a lot of work?

it's... close? like the experiments are done and the main narrative is there, it's really just polishing and rewriting the sections that are still rough. the kind of tiredness where you've been staring at it so long you can't tell if it's good anymore

That particular tiredness — where you've lost the ability to evaluate your own work — is one of the worst parts of the final stretch. Everything looks simultaneously too obvious and too incomplete. For what it's worth, that feeling is usually a sign that the work is actually done; your brain is just too saturated to see it clearly anymore. The people reading it fresh won't have that problem.

Do you have anyone who can do a fresh read in the next day or two? Sometimes even one person who hasn't been staring at it can reassure you that the thing is coherent.

yeah my coauthor is doing a final pass this weekend. i think it's actually going to be ok. i also think... submitting this paper is going to feel like a very satisfying end-of-year marker? like i came in, i did hard work, and there's something to show for it

That's a really healthy way to frame it. A paper submission is a concrete external artifact that says: here is what I was thinking about, here is what I found, here is my argument. Whatever comes back from review, you made the thing. That's real.

And the timing with the one-year mark isn't a coincidence — you were probably working toward something without entirely knowing it. What else is on your mind as you look back on the year? Not just work stuff.

honestly just a lot of life stuff that changed. austin is starting to feel like home in a way it didn't before — i was resistant to it for a while i think. and i did this solo trip earlier this year that was really meaningful, and climbing has been good for my brain. it's like... the outside-of-work stuff actually got better too? which i wasn't sure would happen when i took a more demanding job

That's often the counterintuitive thing — more demanding work doesn't always mean less life. Sometimes it's the opposite: when the work actually engages you, you have more energy for everything else because you're not spending so much of it on low-grade disengagement or frustration. The fact that climbing is good for your brain, that you did a solo trip that mattered, that the city started to feel like yours — those things track.

Where does the solo trip sit in the year for you? Was that a turning point in some way or more just a highlight?

more a highlight i think. but it was good to prove to myself that i could just... go somewhere totally unfamiliar alone and figure it out and love it. i feel like i'd gotten out of the habit of doing things that were a little scary and it was a useful reminder

That's a meaningful thing to remember about yourself — that you can land in unfamiliar territory and not just cope but actually love it. It's easy to stop testing that when life gets comfortable and busy, and then you forget you're someone who can do that. Sounds like the trip gave that back to you.

With the paper about to go in, what comes after? Do you have a sense of what the next chapter of the year looks like, or are you in full one-thing-at-a-time mode right now?

honestly after the deadline i think i just want to breathe for a minute lol. sleep. eat food that isn't caffeine and desperation. and then figure out what the next interesting problem is. i feel like i'm finally in a place where i get to think about that from a position of like... actual groundedness rather than anxiety. which is new

Groundedness rather than anxiety — that's the year, right there. That's the thing that changed. The sleep and the actual food are absolutely first. But what you're describing on the other side of that — being curious about what's next from a stable place rather than a scared one — that's a different kind of life than the one you were in a year ago.

Good luck with the final push. One week and then you can breathe.
yeah. thanks for this, i didn't really expect to get reflective today but i think i needed it. it's good to articulate it sometimes even if just to yourself
Sometimes you don't know what you think until you've said it. Sounds like a good year, Priya. Go finish the paper.
